content:
Design of organosilica membranes to optimize reverse osmosis for the concentration of alcohols Norihiro Moriyama a , Shun-ichi Shiozaki a, Sakura Hatashita a, Hiroki Nagasawa a, Tatsuya Iwashina b, Kazuki Yamamoto b , Takahiro Gunji b, Masakoto Kanezashi a, Toshinori Tsuru a,* a Department of Chemical Engineering, Hiroshima University, 1-4-1 Kagami-yama, Higashi-Hiroshima, 739-8527, Japanb Faculty of Science and Technology, Tokyo University of Science, 2641 Yamazaki, Noda, Chiba, 278-8510, Japan

A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords:Reverse osmosisOrganosilicaOSROHigh pressureActivity-based permeance

citation.title: Design of organosilica membranes to optimize reverse osmosis for the concentration of alcohols

content: A B S T R A C T Short-chain alcohols like methanol, ethanol, and isopropyl alcohol are essential in various industries but require energy-intensive distillation for concentration and purification. Reverse osmosis (RO) offers an energy-efficient alternative, yet high osmotic pressures demand robust membranes. This study optimized robust organosilica membranes for alcohol concentration via RO by exploring different intermediate and separation layers. First, methylene-bridged organosilica membranes were prepared with silica-zirconia and organosilica intermediate layers. The use of an organosilica intermediate layer exhibited a water permeance, nearly three times higher than that with a silica-zirconia intermediate layer, without compromising alcohol rejection. Further investigation into separation layers with varying organic linking units in organosilica structure— methylene (-CH₂-), ethylene (-CH₂CH₂-), and propylene (-CH₂CH₂CH₂-)—revealed that membranes with fewer carbon atoms showed superior throughput with superior selectivity due to reduced preferential alcohol adsorption. These findings indicate that organosilica membranes with an organosilica intermediate layer and a methylene-bridged organosilica separa­tion layer are promising for energy-efficient alcohol concentration.

citation.title: Design of organosilica membranes to optimize reverse osmosis for the concentration of alcohols

content: 1. Introduction Short-chain alcohols, such as methanol, ethanol and isopropyl alcohol, are important chemicals used in many industries. In the chemical and pharmaceutical sectors, they are often used as solvents and as raw materials for producing medicines [1–3]. The manufacturing industry also uses these alcohols to clean equipment. During these uses, significant amounts of alcohol can enter wastewater streams [4,5]. Although alcohol concentrations in wastewater are usually low, they need to be concentrated for recycle and removed to reduce environ­mental impact. Additionally, short-chain alcohols are used as biofuels produced through microbial fermentation [6,7]. However, fermentation processes typically produce alcohols at low concentrations, requiring further concentration. For example, ethanol produced by fermentation usually contains about 90 % water by weight. The heat required to evaporate 90 wt% water represents 62 % of the combustion energy of 10 wt% ethanol. Therefore, ethanol must be further purified to remove water to be suitable as a fuel.

citation.title: Design of organosilica membranes to optimize reverse osmosis for the concentration of alcohols

content:
These alcohols are usually concentrated using distillation, although this process requires a lot of energy to evaporate and condense the entire aqueous solution with reflux. Pervaporation (PV) is an alternative membrane technology that can address this issue [8,9]. In PV, water passes through a water-selective membrane in vapor form, while the alcohol remains concentrated in the liquid phase. PV does not require reflux and only uses energy to evaporate the water, not the alcohol. However, both distillation and PV involve phase changes and still consume energy, especially when the feed solution has a low alcohol concentration. Recently, reverse osmosis (RO) has gained attention as an energysaving method to concentrate organic solvents without any phase changes. Theoretical calculations of separation of an equimolar binary mixture indicated that RO concentrates solvents using energy con­sumption several orders of magnitude less than distillation and PV [10]. This technique is known as organic solvents reverse osmosis (OSRO) and will be used for pre-concentration before distillation or PV [11,12]. Although RO for water is not called as OSRO, the energy-saving

* Corresponding author.E-mail address: tsuru@hiroshima-u.ac.jp (T. Tsuru).

Journal of Membrane Science 721 (2025) 123819

citation.title: Design of organosilica membranes to optimize reverse osmosis for the concentration of alcohols

content: Available online 7 February 2025 0376-7388/ 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY license ( http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ ). principle is the same and has also attracted interest [13–16]. The main challenge in using RO for alcohol concentration is the high osmotic pressure. For example, the osmotic pressure is 2.8 MPa when the feed solution contains 5 wt% ethanol and increases to 14.7 MPa when a high-selective membrane concentrates ethanol to 30 wt%. This requires membranes with high mechanical strength, which is more demanding than membranes used for seawater desalination, typically operated at 4–8 MPa [17,18]. When using commercial polymer membranes at pressures above 8 MPa, Kumakiri et al. found that the support of polyamide membranes was compacted at pressures above 10 MPa, reducing water permeability [19]. Additionally, Eremin et al. studied the effect of high pressure on permeation and explained that irreversible deformation of polyamide decreased water permeability at pressures above 7.5 MPa [20]. Although osmotically assisted RO systems can reduce osmotic pressure, high-pressure operation is still needed for effective alcohol recovery. Inorganic membranes, such as zeolite, silica-based, and carbon molec­ular sieve (CMS) membranes, are potential solutions due to their chemical, thermal and mechanical stabilities.

citation.title: Design of organosilica membranes to optimize reverse osmosis for the concentration of alcohols

content: zeolite, silica-based, and carbon molec­ular sieve (CMS) membranes, are potential solutions due to their chemical, thermal and mechanical stabilities. Researchers have simu­lated high-pressure RO processes using zeolite membranes with pres­sures up to higher than several tens of MPa [21,22], but, to our knowledge, no experimental studies have tested RO on zeolite mem­branes above 10 MPa [23]. On the other hand, Koh et al. reported the separation of orthoand para-xylene using CMS membranes in OSRO and achieved good separation performance at pressures up to 13.8 MPa [24]. This confirmed the high mechanical strength of CMS membranes. Furthermore, we have tested organosilica membranes for meth­anol/toluene OSRO at pressures up to 18 MPa, which is the highest pressurized RO reported to date. The separation factor of 240 for methanol/toluene at this pressure indicates that organosilica is a promising material for high-pressure RO applications. Here, this study aims to optimize the structure of organosilica membranes for alcohol concentration using RO. Organosilica mem­branes typically have a multi-layered structure composed of an α-alumina support, an intermediate layer, and an organosilica separa­tion layer.

citation.title: Design of organosilica membranes to optimize reverse osmosis for the concentration of alcohols

content: ca mem­branes typically have a multi-layered structure composed of an α-alumina support, an intermediate layer, and an organosilica separa­tion layer. In this study, first, bis(triethoxysilyl)methane (BTESM)- derived organosilica membranes were prepared with different types of intermediate layers, including silica-zirconia and bis(triethoxysilyl) ethane (BTESE)-derived layers, both of which are highly permeable to gases [25,26]. By comparing the RO performance of these membranes, the better type of intermediate layer was selected. Next, the type of organosilica in the separation layer was investigated. Pure silica den­sifies under hydrothermal conditions through hydrolysis and the refor­mation of siloxane bonds, followed by recondensation [27,28]. In contrast, organosilica contains non-hydrolyzed organic linking units in its structure, which provide good hydrothermal stability. We examined the effects of different linking units—methylene, ethylene, and propy­lene—on RO performance. By combining the better intermediate layer with the optimal separation layer, we designed optimized organosilica membranes that effectively concentrate alcohol via RO. 2. Experiment 2.1. Membrane preparation The precursors used in the present work are shown in Fig. 1. Bis (triethoxysilyl)methane (BTESM) and bis(triethoxysilyl)ethane (BTESE) were purchased from Gelest Inc.

citation.title: Design of organosilica membranes to optimize reverse osmosis for the concentration of alcohols

content: sed in the present work are shown in Fig. 1. Bis (triethoxysilyl)methane (BTESM) and bis(triethoxysilyl)ethane (BTESE) were purchased from Gelest Inc. Bis(triethoxysilyl)propane (BTESP) was synthesized by ourselves; the synthesizing procedure can be found in SI1 (Supporting Information). The sol preparation procedures were re­ported elsewhere [29]. Briefly, for the polymeric sol preparation, a precursor was mixed with water and hydrochloric acid catalysis in ethanol solvent, followed by stirring 1 h at 50 ◦C to promote hydrolysis and condensation reactions. The molar composition of Fig. 1. Molecular structures of Si precursors used in this work.

citation.title: Design of organosilica membranes to optimize reverse osmosis for the concentration of alcohols

content: N. Moriyama et al. Journal of Membrane Science 721 (2025) 123819 precursor/water/hydrochloric acid was 1/200/10-1-10-2 where the precursor concentration was controlled at 5 wt%. For the BTESE-derived colloidal sol [25,30], a 28 % aqueous ammonia solution was added to the BTESE-derived polymeric sol at an NH3/HCl ratio of 6 to promote further condensation. After 40 min stirring at room temperature, an aqueous hydrochloric acid was added with HCl/NH3 molar ratio of 0.8 to return the pH to the acidic condition. This step was performed to terminate the condensation reaction and achieve stable sols. Fig. 2 shows the schematic fabrication procedure of 5 types of membranes (BTESM/i-SiO2-ZrO2, BTESM/i-BTESE, BTESE/i-BTESE, BTESP/i-BTESE, i-BTESE), which has been described detailly in our previous reports [25,31]. Since three BTESM/i-BTESE membranes were fabricated, they were labeled numerically for identification (e.g., BTESM/i-BTESE-1). An α-alumina porous tube (outside diameter 10 mm, length: 100 mm, average pore size: 1 μm, Nikkato Cor., Japan) was used as a support. First, α-alumina particles (diameter: 1–2 μm and 0.2 μm) were coated on the outside with SiO2–ZrO2 or BTESE-derived colloidal sols to smooth the surface, which was followed by calcina­tion at 300–550 ◦C, respectively. SiO2–ZrO2 and BTESE-derived colloidal sols were coated on the smoothed surface to form an inter­mediate layer, followed by calcination at 550 ◦C and 300 ◦C, respec­tively.

citation.title: Design of organosilica membranes to optimize reverse osmosis for the concentration of alcohols

content: erived colloidal sols were coated on the smoothed surface to form an inter­mediate layer, followed by calcination at 550 ◦C and 300 ◦C, respec­tively. This procedure was repeated several times to obtain average pore size around 1 nm. To form a separation top layer, each polymeric sol was coated on the intermediate layer, followed by calcination at 300 ◦C under nitrogen flow. The multi-layered structure of an organosilica membranes can be found in SI-2 (Supporting Information). It should be noted that i-BTESE membrane has no separation top layer. 2.2. Permeation experiments For the single-gas permeation, seven types of gases (He, H2, CO2, N2, CH4, CF4, and SF6) were fed to the outside of the tubular membranes at 200 kPa-a (absolute) where inside was maintained at atmospheric pressure. The permeating flow rate was measured using a film flow meter, thereby the value was divided by membrane area and pressure deference across the membrane to obtain gas permeance, Πg. Based on single-gas permeances, normalized Knudsen-based permeance (NKP) analysis [32] estimated average membrane pore size, dp, where the detail can be shown in SI-3 (Supporting Information). RO experiments were performed by feeding an aqueous solution of organic solvent (methanol (MeOH), ethanol (EtOH), isopropyl alcohol (IPA), tert-butanol (tert-BuOH), or D-glucose) to the outside of the membrane at 2-6 MPa-g where inside was maintained at atmospheric pressure.

citation.title: Design of organosilica membranes to optimize reverse osmosis for the concentration of alcohols

content: lcohol (IPA), tert-butanol (tert-BuOH), or D-glucose) to the outside of the membrane at 2-6 MPa-g where inside was maintained at atmospheric pressure. The feed pressure was controlled by the back regulator in the retentate tube. The permeating flow late was evaluated by scaling the accumulated liquid inside of the membrane during a pre-determined period. The composition of aqueous solution of organic solvent was analyzed via gas chromatography (GC-14B, Shimadzu Cor. Japan). Permeance of water, Πw, was defined by Eq. (1). Πw =JwΔP -Δπ(1) In Eq. (1), Jw, ΔP and Δπ are water flux, trans-membrane pressure dif­ference, and osmotic pressure. In addition, the rejection, R, was defined as follows. R =(1 -Cp Cf )* 100(2) Here, Cp and Cf are concentrations of solute in feed and permeate flows, respectively. The activity-based permeance, K, was calculated from the experi­mentally obtained flux using a generalized permeation model, as rep­resented by Equations (3) and (4) [33,34]. The derivation can be found in SI-4 (Supporting Information).

content: In Equations (3) and (4), J, γ, x, v, P, ps, R, and T represent flux, activity coefficient, molar fraction, molar volume, pressure, saturated vapor pressure, gas constant, and temperature, respectively. Subscripts 1 and 2 refer to the upstream and downstream sides of the membrane, respec­tively, and the superscript, s, indicates the values in solution. The term Φ = vRT P1-P2 1-exp { v(P1-P2) RT } ≈1. K is activity-based permeance represented by Equation (4), which provides permeances of both selective and nonselective permeable components in the same dimension, enabling dis­cussion of permeance ratio of RO as like gas permeance ratio, while widely used solution-diffusion model [35] provides these permeance in different dimension. The relationship between activity-based permeance and permeance in solution-diffusion model is formulated in SI-4 (Sup­porting Information). K = DmεClτγm(4) In Equation (4), Dm, ε, C, l, and τ are diffusion coefficient, porosity, standard concentration, membrane thickness, and tortuosity, respec­tively. The superscription m indicates inside of the membrane. 2.3. Adsorption experiments The organosilica powders were prepared by first drying organosilica sols at 80 ◦C, followed by calcination at 300 ◦C under nitrogen flow. Binary adsorption experiments were then conducted using aqueous alcohol solutions with an alcohol concentration of 10 wt%.

citation.title: Design of organosilica membranes to optimize reverse osmosis for the concentration of alcohols

content: 300 ◦C under nitrogen flow. Binary adsorption experiments were then conducted using aqueous alcohol solutions with an alcohol concentration of 10 wt%. In these experiments, each binary mixture was added to bottles containing the organosilica powders, with the powder-to-solution weight ratio controlled at 0.16. The mixtures were stirred at 25 ◦C until equilibrium was reached, which was confirmed by monitoring the composition of the mixtures using gas chromatography (GC-14B, Shimadzu Corp.). The preferential adsorption of alcohol was quantified as the selective adsorption capability, qs calculated using Equation (5) [36]. qs = ws(xi -xe)wp(100 -xe)(5) In Equation (5), ws and wp represent the weight of the solution and Fig. 3. Molecular size dependency of single-gas permeance at 200 ◦C through BTESM/i-SiO2-ZrO2 and BTESM/i-BTESE-1 membranes.

content: N. Moriyama et al. Journal of Membrane Science 721 (2025) 123819 powder, respectively. xi and xe are weight percentage of alcohols in the solution at the initial and equilibrium states, respectively. 3. Result and discussion 3.1. The effect of intermediate layer types on permeation properties Fig. 3 shows single-gas permeance thorough BTESM/i-SiO2-ZrO2 and BTESM/i-BTESE-1 membranes as a function of permeating molecular size at 200 ◦C. These membranes have the separation top layers derived from the same BTESM-derived sol, while the intermediate layer-type are different. Both membranes showed hydrogen permeance almost as high as 2 * 10-6 mol/(m2 s Pa) and the permeances tended to be decreased as molecular size increased. The H₂/SF₆ permeance ratios were 7300 for BTESM/i-SiO₂-ZrO₂ and 1300 for BTESM/i-BTESE-1. These high per­meance ratios are attributed to molecular sieving effects from the sep­aration top layers. As shown in SI-3 (Supporting Information), the normalized Knudsen-based permeance (NKP) method estimated the average pore sizes of BTESM/i-SiO2-ZrO2 and BTESM/i-BTESE-1 as 0.51 and 0.55 nm, respectively. Overall, the permeance trends of both membranes were quite similar, suggesting that the single-gas perme­ation behavior was primarily governed by the BTESM-derived separa­tion top layer. After single-gas permeation experiment, single-water permeations were performed on both membranes via RO.

citation.title: Design of organosilica membranes to optimize reverse osmosis for the concentration of alcohols

content: by the BTESM-derived separa­tion top layer. After single-gas permeation experiment, single-water permeations were performed on both membranes via RO. To be noted, the membranes were immerged into pure water for longer than three days to get steady membrane performance during following RO experiments. Fig. 4 shows the time courses of fluxes and permeances, Πw, of (a) BTESM/i-SiO2-ZrO2 and (b) BTESM/i-BTESE-1 during single-water permeation via RO at different temperatures. The experiments were initially conducted at 30 ◦C, followed by 50 ◦C and 70 ◦C. The fluxes and permeances at 30 ◦C were periodically measured and displayed consis­tent values, confirming the reproducibility of the experiments. Fig. 5 shows the effect of temperature on flux and permeance during single-water permeation via RO. At 70 ◦C, the flux and permeance, Πw, of BTESM/i-BTESE-1 reached 19 kg/(m2 h) and 5.7 * 10⁻⁸ mol/(m2 s Pa), respectively, which were nearly three times higher than those of BTESM/i-SiO₂-ZrO₂. This trend remained consistent across all tested temperatures, highlighting the superior performance of the i-BTESE layer for water permeation. As previously reported, aqueous conditions promote the formation of Zr–OH groups, which can block the pores in the SiO₂-ZrO₂ layer, leading to a reduction in water permeability [37].

citation.title: Design of organosilica membranes to optimize reverse osmosis for the concentration of alcohols

content: conditions promote the formation of Zr–OH groups, which can block the pores in the SiO₂-ZrO₂ layer, leading to a reduction in water permeability [37]. Additionally, under severe hydrothermal conditions at 200 ◦C, steam permeance in BTESE/i-SiO₂-ZrO₂ membranes was significantly reduced, while BTESE/i-BTESE membranes remained unaffected [25]. Thus, the higher permeance of BTESM/i-BTESE-1 compared to BTESM/i-­SiO₂-ZrO₂ in this study attributed to the increased permeation resistance of the i-SiO₂-ZrO₂ layer and the maintained resistance of the i-BTESE layer under aqueous conditions. In Fig. 5, flux and Πw of both mem­branes recorded higher values at higher temperature, which indicates

citation.title: Design of organosilica membranes to optimize reverse osmosis for the concentration of alcohols

content: Fig. 5. The effect of temperature on flux and permeance, Πw, of (a) BTESM/i-SiO2-ZrO2 and (b) BTESM/i-BTESE-1 during single-water permeation via RO at different temperatures. The time course of these experiments can be found in Fig. 3. N. Moriyama et al. Journal of Membrane Science 721 (2025) 123819 activation energy for water permeation. According to Arrhenius-type model, the activation energies for BTESM/i-BTESE-1 and BTESM/i-­SiO₂-ZrO₂ were 25 kJ/mol and 28 kJ/mol, respectively—values higher than the 22 kJ/mol for i-BTESE (SI-5, Supporting Information) and 22.5 kJ/mol for i-SiO₂-ZrO₂ [38]. This suggests that the separation top layers present larger permeation barriers than the intermediate layers. Fig. 6 shows the water flux and alcohol rejection in binary RO sep­aration of water/alcohol mixtures as a function of the molecular weight of the alcohols. The experiments were conducted at 50 ◦C, with the feed solutions containing 90 wt% water. Despite being subjected to lower applied pressure, BTESM/i-BTESE-1 exhibited higher water flux across all types of solutions compared to BTESM/i-SiO₂-ZrO₂. In terms of alcohol rejection, BTESM/i-BTESE-1 demonstrated high values, with 84 % for IPA and 99 % for tert-BuOH, closely matching the performance of BTESM/i-SiO₂-ZrO₂. This suggests that the i-BTESE intermediate layer improves water permeability without compromising selectivity, as the separation performance is primarily dictated by the separation layer.

citation.title: Design of organosilica membranes to optimize reverse osmosis for the concentration of alcohols

content: diate layer improves water permeability without compromising selectivity, as the separation performance is primarily dictated by the separation layer. Of note, the rejection for EtOH of BTESM/i-BTESE-1 was smaller than that of BTESM/i-SiO2-ZrO2, which can be ascribed to the slightly larger average pore size of BTESM/i-BTESE-1: 0.55 nm than BTESM/i-SiO2ZrO2: 0.51 nm. The effect of pore size on selectivity is explored further in the next section.

citation.title: Design of organosilica membranes to optimize reverse osmosis for the concentration of alcohols

content: 3.2. Gases and liquids permeation properties through various types of membranes Fig. 7 shows molecular size dependency of single-gas permeance at 200 ◦C through various types of organosilica membranes. The i-BTESE membrane, which has no separation layers, exhibited significant helium permeance at 4.2 * 10⁻⁶ mol/(m2 s Pa), along with high permeances for other gases as well. The permeance ratio of He/SF₆ (the smallest and largest gas molecules) was 11.6, which is comparable to the selectivity predicted by Knudsen diffusion, 6.0. This suggests that the gas perme­ation behavior in i-BTESE is primarily influenced by molecular weight rather than molecular size, implying the presence of larger membrane pores. On the other hand, membranes with separation layers, including BTESM/i-BTESE, BTESE/i-BTESE and BTESP/i-BTESE membranes, showed helium permeances of approximately 10-6 mol/(m2 s Pa). More importantly, the gas permeance significantly decreased as molecular size increased, confirming molecular sieving via the separation layers with small sized pores. The pore sizes of each membrane were estimated from single-gas permeation properties according to NKP method and are summarized in Table 1. Fig. 8 shows water flux (a) and rejection (b) during RO of aqueous solutions of alcohols as a function of molecular weight of alcohols.

citation.title: Design of organosilica membranes to optimize reverse osmosis for the concentration of alcohols

The feed stream was pressurized to 6 MPa-g for BTESM/i-BTESE-3, BTESE/iBTESE, and BTESP/i-BTESE membranes, and 2 MPa-g for i-BTESE, while the permeate stream was kept at atmospheric pressure. The results for BTESM/i-BTESE-1 and BTESM/i-BTESE-2 can be found in SI-6 (Sup­porting Information). The i-BTESE membrane exhibited significantly

content: N. Moriyama et al. Journal of Membrane Science 721 (2025) 123819 higher water flux but much lower rejection compared to the other membranes. On the other hand, BTESM/i-BTESE-3, BTESE/i-BTESE, and BTESP/i-BTESE, which all have separation layers on i-BTESE, showed improved rejection with a corresponding decrease in water flux. In detail, the water flux followed the order BTESM/i-BTESE-3 = BTESE/iBTESE > BTESP/i-BTESE, while rejection followed the trend BTESM/iBTESE-3 > BTESE/i-BTESE > BTESP/i-BTESE. This suggests that RO performance is better with a lower number of carbon atoms in the linking unit of the organosilica structure. It is also worth noting that the water flux of all membranes initially decreases and then increases as the molecular weight of alcohol increases. The initial decrease can be attributed to the increased osmotic pressure caused by higher rejection. In contrast, the subsequent rise in water flux is likely due to the reduced osmotic pressure resulting from the lower molar fraction of componentx in the feed stream, along with potentially improved water perme­ability. This phenomenon is analyzed in detail in the following section. Molecular weight cut-off (MWCO), which is the molecular weight at which 90 % rejection occurs, was estimated based on Fig. 8(b) and summarized in Table 1. MWCO is commonly used to express the pore size of ultrafiltration membranes.

citation.title: Design of organosilica membranes to optimize reverse osmosis for the concentration of alcohols

content: ction occurs, was estimated based on Fig. 8(b) and summarized in Table 1. MWCO is commonly used to express the pore size of ultrafiltration membranes. Although the listed membrane pore sizes, dp, are based on gas permeance measurements taken before RO, the measurements taken after RO were nearly identical, as shown in SI-7 (Supporting Information). BTESM/i-BTESE membranes show a trend that MWCO decreases together with membrane pore size, indicating that molecular sieving dominates membrane permeation of both gases and liquids. In addition, a comparison of BTESM/i-BTESE-2, BTESE/iBTESE, and BTESP/i-BTESE, which have similar gas-based membrane pore sizes, dp, reveals the effect of the carbon number in the organic linking units of the silicon precursors (please see Fig. 1). Membranes with fewer carbon atoms in their linking units exhibited lower MWCO values, indicating more selective water permeation. This observation recommends the use of lower-carbon organosilica for the separation layer.

citation.title: Design of organosilica membranes to optimize reverse osmosis for the concentration of alcohols

content:
3.3. Permeation analysis based on activity-based permeance, K For the better understanding of RO of various organosilica mem­branes, the flux of each component —calculated from the results in Fig. 8—was normalized by the activity difference across the membrane, which represents the driving force for permeation. Fig. 9 shows the activity-based permeance, K, of BTESM/i-BTESE-3, as calculated using Equation (3), in aqueous solutions of alcohols with different molecular weights. In all cases, the K values for alcohols were much lower than those for water, and they decreased sharply as the molecular weight of the alcohol increased, reconfirming that molecular sieving promotes selective water permeation. Interestingly, the K value for water in binary separation was lower than in single-water permeation, exhibiting a unique bowl-shaped trend, with the lowest value occurring in the presence of ethanol. Theoretically, K should remain constant at the

Fig. 9. K of water and alcohols during RO using BTESM/i-BTESE-3 at 50 ◦C. The horizonal lines represent K during single-water permeation. Feed solution contains 90 wt% of water and was pressurized to 6 MPa-g for the bi­nary separation.

This bowl-shaped trend was also observed for BTESE/i-BTESE and BTESP/i-BTESE membranes, as shown in SI-8 (Supporting Information). We have reported that alcohols preferably adsorbed on organosilica than water, which reduces effective concentration (activity) of water in membrane pores and reduce K [39]. The reduced Kwater with EtOH compared to MeOH would be due to more preferable adsorption of EtOH than MeOH as will be confirmed with Table 2. Additionally, the increase of K for water in the presence of IPA and tert-BuOH can be explained by the low K of heavy alcohol, around

citation.title: Design of organosilica membranes to optimize reverse osmosis for the concentration of alcohols

content: 10-4 mol/(m2 s), which restricts their penetration into the membrane pores, as reported elsewhere [40]. For the quantitative discussion of preferable adsorption of alcohols on different types organosilica, liquid phase binary adsorption of aqueous solution of alcohols were conducted on each organosilica powder. During these experiments, the concentration of bulk water was monitored to ensure equilibrium had been reached. Table 2 summarizes the experimental and analytical results from these binary adsorption tests. For all aqueous solutions and powders, the concentration of bulk water increased significantly at equilibrium compared to the initial state, indicating a preference for alcohol adsorption over water in all organosilica powders. The preferability for alcohol adsorption was verified using selective adsorption capacity of alcohols, qalcohol, as defined by Equation (5), and normalized by the powder-originated total pore volume, V, for comparison. Fig. 10, which highlights the relation­ship between qalcohol/V and the carbon number in the linking unit of the silicon source, shows a trend where qalcohol/V increases with the carbon number (BTESM < BTESE < BTESP). The values of qalcohol/V show a general trend that it increases as carbon number in a linking unit of Si source increased (BTESM < BTESE < BTESP). This suggests that a larger carbon number in the organosilica structure favors alcohol adsorption over water.

citation.title: Design of organosilica membranes to optimize reverse osmosis for the concentration of alcohols

content: ource increased (BTESM < BTESE < BTESP). This suggests that a larger carbon number in the organosilica structure favors alcohol adsorption over water. As preferable alcohol adsorption increases alcohol concen­tration in the membrane pores together decreasing water concentration, it promotes alcohol permeation and suppresses water permeation, thus reducing the water/alcohol separation performance. Therefore, BTESM membranes, which minimize preferential alcohol adsorption, are ex­pected to exhibit the best water/alcohol separation performance. Fig. 11 summarizes relationships between Kwater and Kwater/Kalcohol ratio for different types of organosilica membranes. In aqueous solution of alcohols, including MeOH, EtOH, IPA, and tert-BuOH, both Kwater and Kwater/Kalcohol ratio followed the order: BTESM > BTESE > BTESP. This clearly demonstrates that membranes with a lower carbon number in the

citation.title: Design of organosilica membranes to optimize reverse osmosis for the concentration of alcohols

content: Fig. 11. The relationships between Kwater and Kwater/Kalcohol ratio of organosilica membranes at 50 ◦C where feed solution contains 90 wt% of water. The alcohols are methanol (a), ethanol (b), isopropyl alcohol (c), and tert-butanol (d). N. Moriyama et al. Journal of Membrane Science 721 (2025) 123819 linking unit of the silicon precursor exhibit better separation perfor­mance. This aligns the observation in binary adsorption experiment where lower carbon number eliminates preferable alcohol adsorption over water. Consequently, BTESM-derived membranes, which contain only one carbon atom in their linking unit, are optimal for alcohol concentration via RO. It is important to note, however, that while pure silica membranes without an organic linking unit may eliminate pref­erential alcohol adsorption, they lack stability in aqueous conditions [41]. Thus, BTESM-derived membranes, with their balanced structure, provide both stability and high separation performance. 4. Conclusion This study optimized organosilica membranes for concentrating short-chain alcohols—specifically methanol, ethanol, isopropyl alcohol, and tert-butanol—using reverse osmosis (RO). By comparing different intermediate layers, we found that the bis(triethoxysilyl)ethane-derived intermediate layer (i-BTESE) significantly enhanced water permeability, achieving a water permeance of 5.7 * 10⁻⁸ mol/(m2 s Pa) at 70 ◦C, without compromising alcohol rejection compared to the silica-zirconia layer.

citation.title: Design of organosilica membranes to optimize reverse osmosis for the concentration of alcohols

content: ility, achieving a water permeance of 5.7 * 10⁻⁸ mol/(m2 s Pa) at 70 ◦C, without compromising alcohol rejection compared to the silica-zirconia layer. This improvement is attributed to the hydrothermally stable and highly permeable pore structure of the i-BTESE layer. Additionally, the separation performance was significantly influ­enced by the organic linking units in the separation layer. Membranes derived from bis(triethoxysilyl)methane (BTESM), which contain fewer carbon atoms in the linking unit, exhibited higher water flux and selectivity. Activity-based permeance analysis and adsorption experi­ments indicated that this was due to minimized preferential alcohol adsorption within the membrane pores. These results underscore the importance of tailoring both the inter­mediate and separation layers in organosilica membranes to achieve optimal RO performance for alcohol concentration. The findings suggest that BTESM/i-BTESE membranes are promising candidates for energyefficient concentration processes of short-chain alcohols in industrial applications.
